coredata.dc:description: Developing sustainable elastomers derived from natural resources is crucial to mitigate environmental pollution yet challenging on account of their limited properties. In this work, chitin, lignin, and plant oil were selected as the feedstocks to design renewable bottlebrush copolymer elastomers via homogeneous reversible addition-fragmentation chain transfer (RAFT) polymerization. In such kind of bottlebrush copolymers, chitin serves as the rigid backbone, while the plant oil-derived monomer 4-vinylbenzyl oleate (VBO) and lignin-derived monomer syringaldehyde acrylate (SA) were chosen as the soft and hard segments for the rubbery brushes, respectively. These chitin-graft-poly(4-vinylbenzyl oleate-co-syringaldehyde acrylate) (Chitin-g-P(VBO-co-SA)) bottlebrush elastomers possess tunable mechanical properties and superior UV-shielding performance. To further improve their macroscopic behavior, carbon dots (CDs) were incorporated into the Chitin-g-P(VBO-co-SA) copolymers. The resulting Chitin-g-P(VBO-co-SA)/CD composites exhibit significantly enhanced tensile strength and excellent photothermal conversion ability owing to the introduction of CDs. This robust and convenient strategy can inspire the continuous development of strong and functional sustainable elastomers by combining different natural resources.

head.ce:title: Combination of chitin, lignin, and plant oil for high-performance sustainable elastomers with UV-shielding and photothermal properties

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Developing sustainable elastomers derived from natural resources is crucial to mitigate environmental pollution yet challenging on account of their limited properties. In this work, chitin, lignin, and plant oil were selected as the feedstocks to design renewable bottlebrush copolymer elastomers via homogeneous reversible addition-fragmentation chain transfer (RAFT) polymerization. In such kind of bottlebrush copolymers, chitin serves as the rigid backbone, while the plant oil-derived monomer 4-vinylbenzyl oleate (VBO) and lignin-derived monomer syringaldehyde acrylate (SA) were chosen as the soft and hard segments for the rubbery brushes, respectively. These chitin-graft-poly(4-vinylbenzyl oleate-co-syringaldehyde acrylate) (Chitin-g-P(VBO-co-SA)) bottlebrush elastomers possess tunable mechanical properties and superior UV-shielding performance. To further improve their macroscopic behavior, carbon dots (CDs) were incorporated into the Chitin-g-P(VBO-co-SA) copolymers. The resulting Chitin-g-P(VBO-co-SA)/CD composites exhibit significantly enhanced tensile strength and excellent photothermal conversion ability owing to the introduction of CDs. This robust and convenient strategy can inspire the continuous development of strong and functional sustainable elastomers by combining different natural resources.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: The data that has been used is confidential.

ce:section.ce:para: With the rapid advancement in materials science and the growing concern for environmental issues, there is a significant focus on harnessing biobased feedstock to create robust and sustainable materials, attracting considerable attention from both academic and industrial sectors [1–6]. In recent years, natural resources such as cellulose, lignin, hemicellulose, chitin, and plant oils have been proposed to develop innovative renewable materials with adjustable mechanical properties and distinctive functionalities [7–16]. Chitin, as the second most abundant biopolymer in nature, can be derived from various sources including shrimp shells, crab shells, shellfish, fungi, and other organisms [17]. Beyond its commendable mechanical performance, chitin exhibits non-toxicity, exceptional biocompatibility, and remarkable biodegradability [18]. These inherent advantages have led to the widespread applications of chitin in different fields, including elastomers, bioplastics, packaging materials, cosmetics, adhesives, tissue engineering, and pharmaceuticals [19–25].

Thermoplastic elastomers (TPEs) are highly coveted for their unique mechanical properties and extensive utility in automotive, wires, coatings, bioengineering, electronics, and damping materials [26–30]. Nevertheless, the majority of TPEs are still synthesized with non-renewable petroleum-based monomers, which may cause environmental pollution. To mitigate carbon emissions and advance toward carbon neutrality, there is a growing emphasis on developing TPEs derived from renewable materials [31]. High-performance biobased elastomers can be achieved by adjusting their compositions and network structures using sustainable resources, thereby meeting the diverse application demands across various fields [32–37]. Lignin, composed of phenylpropanoid units linked by carbon‑carbon and ether bonds, stands out as the predominant natural aromatic polymer with the potential to yield high-value-added fuels, chemicals, and biobased materials [38–40]. As a primary byproduct of biorefineries and paper industries, lignin holds great potential for reducing the consumption of fossil resources and mitigating pollution. Syringyl, guaiacyl, and p-hydroxyphenyl groups are typical phenylpropane units presented in lignin, which can be transformed into phenol derivatives after depolymerization and upgrading, such as syringaldehyde, guaiacol, vanillin, and eugenol [41]. Consequently, a diverse range of lignin-based acrylic and methacrylic monomers can be synthesized via esterification utilizing the aforementioned phenols [42]. Homopolymers derived from these sustainable monomers demonstrate elevated glass transition temperatures and robust thermal stability, making them ideal rigid components for biobased elastomers [43].

Formed by glycerin and fatty acids, plant oils can substitute partial fossil feedstock when designing sustainable polymers. The presence of long alkyl chains and carbon‑carbon double bonds in fatty acids endows plant oil-based polymers with low glass transition temperatures and outstanding hydrophobicity [44]. Oleic acid (OA), a typical fatty acid containing 18 carbon atoms and a double bond between the 9th and 10th carbon atoms, can be transformed into polymerizable monomers through esterification, transvinylation, acrylamidation, bromoacetoxylation, and epoxy ring opening reactions [45]. The combination of lignin and plant oil-derived monomers can offer new opportunities for renewable elastomers. However, plant oil-based elastomers exhibit limited tensile strength and extensibility due to their high entanglement molecular weights [46]. Hence, it is necessary and advantageous to introduce additional entanglements by meticulously manipulating the chemical compositions and network structures through robust polymerization and post-polymerization techniques.

In general, the mechanical properties of TPEs are mainly influenced by their topologies, microphase-separated morphologies, and covalent/noncovalent interactions [47–50]. Bottlebrush elastomers, characterized by a single backbone and densely grafted side chains, exhibit distinctive mechanical responses and intriguing rheological behavior. Typically, the mechanical performance of these elastomers can be facilely adjusted by tuning the length of the backbone, the molecular weights of the side chains, and the spacing of branching points [51–53]. Notably, bottlebrush elastomers have shown significantly enhanced tensile strength compared to their linear counterparts, attributed to their bottlebrush architecture [54–60]. As a facile and controlled polymerization technique, reversible addition-fragmentation chain transfer (RAFT) polymerization has been widely used to synthesize polymers with complex topological structures and narrow distributions [61,62]. Compared to traditional linear copolymers, chitin graft copolymers can exhibit higher tensile strength and Young's modulus owing to the introduction of chitin as the rigid backbone [63]. Recently, we found that the mechanical performance of Chitin-graft-poly(vanillin acrylate-co-lauryl acrylate) (Chitin-g-P(VA-co-LA)) copolymers can be further improved by the incorporation of dynamic network structures formed by 1,5-diaminopentane and the aldehyde groups in VA repeating units [64]. Therefore, the combination of chitin and renewable monomers through RAFT polymerization can inspire the development of novel sustainable bottlebrush materials derived from different biomass resources.

In this work, sustainable chitin-graft-poly(4-vinylbenzyl oleate-co-syringaldehyde acrylate) (Chitin-g-P(VBO-co-SA)) bottlebrush elastomers with varied compositions were prepared via homogeneous RAFT polymerization as illustrated in Fig. 1 . In this design, rigid chitin chain serves as the backbone, while the plant oil derived monomer VBO and lignin-derived monomer SA are used as the soft and hard segments for the random copolymerized brushes, respectively. It is anticipated that these chitin-based bottlebrush elastomers can exhibit tunable mechanical properties by adjusting the chitin and PSA contents. To further enhance their tensile strength and impart photothermal response to these biobased bottlebrush elastomers, carbon dots (CDs) were introduced as nanofillers via a coprecipitation strategy. The impacts of composition, chitin content, and CD content on the properties of the resulting bottlebrush elastomers were comprehensively investigated through various measurements. The integration of chitin with other biobased resources offers promising avenues for the development of high-performance biobased elastomers with specific functions.

Chitin powder from shrimp shells (practical grade, Aladdin) with a viscometric molecular weight of 5.8 × 105 g/mol and deacetylation degree of 4 % was dried at 80 °C overnight before use. 1-Butanethiol (97 %), triethylamine (TEA, 99 %), carbon disulfide (CS2, 99.9 %), 2-bromopropionyl bromide (97 %), 1-allyl-3-methylimidazolium bromide (AMIMBr, 97 %), oleic acid (OA, 99.7 %), 4-vinylbenzyl chloride (VBC, 90 %), 1,1,3,3-tetramethylguanidine (TMG, 99 %), citric acid (CA, 99 %), urea (99 %), syringaldehyde (99 %), acryloyl chloride (96 %), recrystallized 2,2′-azobis(2-methylpropionitrile) (AIBN, 99 %), N,N-dimethylformamide (DMF, 99.5 %), 1,4-dioxane (99.5 %), dichloromethane (DCM, 99.7 %), tetrahydrofuran (THF, 99.5 %), and dimethyl sulfoxide (DMSO, 99.5 %) purchased from Aladdin co., Ltd. (Shanghai, China) were used as received. The chitin-based macroinitiator (Chitin-Br) with a degree of substitution (DS) of 1.83 was prepared by the reaction between chitin and 2-bromopropionyl bromide in AMIMBr as reported [63,65]. S-1-dodecyl-S′-(α,α'-dimethyl-α″-acetic acid) trithiocarbonate (DDMAT) was synthesized according to the reported method [66]. Other analytical reagents were used without any purification.

Chitin-Br (4.0 g, 16.27 mmol of Br) and DMSO (80 mL) were added into a flask, and the mixture was heated at 40 °C with stirring until Chitin-Br was fully dissolved. 1-Butanethiol (0.41 g, 4.41 mmol), TEA (0.46 g, 4.50 mmol), and CS2 (1.04 g, 13.65 mmol) were dissolved in 10 mL of DMSO and stirred for 30 min at room temperature. Thereafter, the above two solutions were mixed and heated at 40 °C with stirring overnight. The resulting solution was dialyzed against deionized water using a 500 Da cut-off bag. The light yellow solid product, Chitin-CTA, was collected and dried at 60 °C under vacuum for 24 h.

OA (42.92 g, 151.5 mmol), VBC (25.44 g, 150 mmol), and DMF (90 mL) were introduced into a round-bottom flask with stirring, and then TMG (17.63 g, 151.5 mmol) was added. After heating at 40 °C for 24 h, the solution was diluted with DCM (500 mL) and washed with saturated sodium chloride solution three times. The organic phase was collected and dried with anhydrous sodium sulfate. After removing the solvent (DCM) under reduced pressure, the product VBO (50.92 g, 85.1 % yield) was obtained.

Syringaldehyde (55.2 g, 300 mmol) and TEA (33.73 g, 330 mmol) were dissolved in 300 mL of DCM, and then acryloyl chloride (31.12 g, 330 mmol) was added dropwise at 0 °C (ice/water bath). After reacting at room temperature for 12 h, the mixture was filtered to remove the formed salt, and the filtrate was washed thoroughly with saturated sodium bicarbonate aqueous solution, saturated sodium chloride solution, and water. The organic phase was collected and dried with anhydrous sodium sulfate. After the removal of DCM under reduced pressure, the product SA (59.78 g, 84.4 % yield) was obtained.

CA (1.152 g, 5.94 mmol) and urea (1.2 g, 19.79 mmol) were dissolved in DMF (18.8 mL). The solution was transferred into a 50 mL Teflon-lined stainless steel autoclave and heated at 160 °C for 12 h. The resulting mixture was centrifuged at 12000 rpm for 10 min, and the supernatant was precipitated into a petroleum ether/ethyl acetate (3/1 by volume) mixture. The obtained CDs were collected and dried at 70 °C under vacuum for 24 h.

Typically, Chitin-CTA (20.1 mg, 0.024 mmol of trithiocarbonate groups), VBO (5.187 g, 13.0 mmol), SA (1.652 g, 7 mmol), AIBN (1.3 mg, 0.008 mmol), 1,4-dioxane (10 mL), and DMF (4 mL) were introduced into a 25 mL Schlenk flask. The flask was sealed and put into a preheated oil bath set at 70 °C after three freeze-pump-thaw cycles. After 24 h, the polymerization was stopped and the solution was poured into 250 mL of methanol to precipitate the product. The resulting Chitin-g-P(VBO-co-SA) copolymer was dried at 60 °C under vacuum for 12 h. Chitin-g-P(VBO-co-SA) copolymers with different VBO/SA feed ratios and varied chitin contents were prepared similarly.

For comparison purpose, a linear P(VBO-co-SA) copolymer was prepared. DDMAT (20.1 mg, 0.055 mmol), VBO (4.788 g, 12.0 mmol), SA (1.888 g, 8 mmol), AIBN (1.3 mg, 0.008 mmol), 1,4-dioxane (10 mL), and DMF (4 mL) were introduced into a 25 mL Schlenk flask. The flask was sealed and put into a preheated oil bath set at 70 °C after three freeze-pump-thaw cycles. After 24 h, the polymerization was stopped and the solution was poured into 250 mL of methanol to precipitate the product. The resulting P(VBO-co-SA) copolymer was dried at 60 °C under vacuum overnight.

Briefly, Chitin-g-P(VBO-co-SA) copolymer (2.0 g) was dissolved in THF (20 mL), while CDs (40 mg) were dispersed in 10 mL of DMF. The above two solutions were mixed and stirred for 12 h at 30 °C. The mixture was precipitated in methanol (300 mL), and the product was dried at 60 °C under vacuum for 12 h. Chitin-g-P(VBO-co-SA)@CD composites with varied CD contents were prepared similarly.

The Fourier transform infrared (FT-IR) and proton nuclear magnetic resonance (NMR) spectra were recorded on Nicolei 6670 FT-IR spectrometer and Agilent DD2 600 MHz NMR spectrometer, respectively. XD-6 X-ray diffraction (Beijing Purkinje General Instrument Co, Ltd.) was selected to confirm the crystal structure of samples. The number-averaged molecular weights (M n), weight-averaged molecular weights (M w), and polydispersities (Đ) of samples were recorded on an Agilent Technologies 1260 Infinity II HPLC system with THF as the eluent. The obtained GPC data were calibrated with polystyrene standard. TA 55 thermogravimetric analyzer (TGA) was used to investigate the decomposition behavior. 5.0 mg of sample was heated from 30 to 700 °C within a nitrogen atmosphere at a heat rate of 10 °C/min. The glass transition temperatures (T gs) of chitin graft copolymers were determined by TA 250 differential scanning calorimeter (DSC) from −60 to 150 °C with a heating rate of 10 °C/min after removing the thermal history. Monotonic and cyclic tensile tests were carried out on a Suns UTM2502 universal testing machine with a tensile speed of 50 mm/min. The films used for tensile tests with a thickness of 0.5 mm were prepared by hot pressing the as-prepared samples at 120 °C for 5 min. The UV–vis spectra in the range of 200–800 nm were recorded on a Shimadzu UV-2550 UV–vis spectrometer. The microstructure morphology was investigated by small-angle X-ray scattering (SAXS) measurements at the synchrotron beamline BL16B1 at Shanghai Synchrotron Radiation Facility (SSRF), China. The wavelength of X-ray and the sample-to-detector distance were 0.124 nm and 1983 mm, respectively. The photothermal behavior was examined by MDL-III-808 near-infrared (NIR) laser, and the temperature values during NIR irradiation were recorded on UNI-T U803 multimeter. The dynamic light scattering (DLS) measurements were carried out on a Malvern ZSU3200 DLS spectrophotometer.

As shown in Fig. 2a, Chitin-CTA displays a new absorption peaked at 1736 cm−1 corresponding to the CO stretching vibration, indicating the successful esterification between chitin and 2-bromopropionyl bromide. Fig. S1a displays the 1H NMR spectrum of Chitin-Br in DMSO‑d 6, the signals located at 2.0–1.4 ppm are ascribed to the methyl protons of 2-bromopropionyl and acetyl groups, while the signals in the range of 8.1–7.7 ppm are attributed to the NH protons of chitin. The DS of Chitin-CTA was determined to be 0.6 according to the 1H NMR spectrum of Chitin-CTA exhibited in Fig. S1b. As illustrated in Fig. 2b, pristine chitin shows typical (020) and (110) planes, suggesting the α-chitin structure [67]. However, the diffraction patterns disappear in Chitin-CTA, which implies that the modification of chitin disrupts the crystalline structure of chitin. Fig. 2c shows the 1H NMR spectra of VBO and SA. For VBO, the signals located in the range of 7.45–7.28 ppm are attributed to the benzene ring protons, and the signals appear at 6.71, 5.74, and 5.25 ppm are ascribed to the protons of CH2 CH groups. The signal peaked at 0.87 ppm is assigned to the protons of methyl end group of VBO. For SA, the signals located at 9.96, 7.35, and 3.85 ppm are assigned to the protons of aldehyde, benzene ring, and methoxy groups, respectively, while the peaks appear in the range of 6.8–6.0 ppm are ascribed to the protons of CH2 CH groups. Given the apparent success of the preparation of Chitin-CTA and sustainable monomers derived from OA and syringaldehyde, Chitin-g-P(VBO-co-SA) copolymers with different VBO/SA feed ratios and chitin contents were synthesized via RAFT polymerization as summarized in Table 1 . The 1H NMR spectra of Chitin-g-P(VBO-co-SA) copolymers are exhibited in Figs. 2d and S2, it can be observed that the VBO/SA ratio in chitin graft copolymer can be determined by using the integral values of methyl protons (0.95–0.70 ppm) and methoxy protons (4.0–3.2 ppm) corresponding to the VBO and SA repeating units, respectively. The detailed molecular weights and polydispersities of Chitin-g-P(VBO-co-SA) and linear P(VBO-co-SA) copolymers are summarized in Table S1.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Thermal behavior of Chitin-CTA and Chitin-g-P(VBO-co-SA) copolymers
ce:section.ce:para:
The thermal properties of polymeric materials are vital in industrial production and practical applications. TGA was applied to investigate the stability of Chitin-g-P(VBO-co-SA) copolymers at elevated temperatures under nitrogen atmosphere. Fig. 3
a and b depict the TGA and DTG curves of pristine chitin, Chitin-CTA, and Chitin-g-P(VBO-co-SA) copolymers, respectively. The onset degradation temperature (T d 5%) and maximum degradation temperature (T d max) of chitin are 256.4 °C and 388.8 °C, respectively, which are much higher than those of Chitin-CTA. For Chitin-CTA, the modification not only broke the original ordered structure of chitin but also introduced unstable 2-bromopropionyloxy and trithiocarbonate derivatives, leading to decreased stability. The T d 5% and T d max of Chitin0.9-PSA22.1 are 303.5 °C and 359.8 °C, respectively, suggesting that Chitin-g-P(VBO-co-SA) copolymers are thermally stable in common applications. DSC measurements were carried out to determine glass transition temperatures (T gs) of these chitin-based copolymers. Fig. 3c depicts the DSC heat flow curves of Chitin-g-P(VBO-co-SA) copolymers prepared with different VBO/SA feed ratios but similar chitin contents, in which the T g values increases from19.6 to 54.6 °C as the PSA content from 20.2 to 29.7 wt%. Since the chitin contents in these chitin graft copolymers are quite similar, the changes in T g values are strongly related to the PSA content. While for Chitin-g-P(VBO-co-SA) copolymers prepared with the same VBO/SA feed ratio but varied chitin contents, as exhibited in Fig. 3d, as the chitin content increases from 0.9 to 8.0 wt%, the T g value grows from 38.6 to 47.5 °C. Although Chitin8.0-PSA20.8 possesses the lowest PSA content, it shows the highest T g value, implying that the T g values are mainly determined by the chitin content for chitin graft copolymers prepared with the same VBO/SA feed ratio. For linear P(VBO-co-SA) copolymer counterpart, it shows the lowest T g value of 36.7 °C as illustrated in Fig. S3 because of the absence of chitin. From the above results, it can be inferred that both the rigid SA segments and chitin backbone can suppress the mobility of grafted brushes, and the increase of chitin or PSA content leads to high T g values. Therefore, the T g values of these Chitin-g-P(VBO-co-SA) copolymers can be systematically adjusted by the PSA and chitin contents.

SAXS measurements were performed to investigate the microstructure of Chitin-g-P(VBO-co-SA) copolymers. Fig. 4a displays the 2D SAXS patterns of Chitin0.9-PSA22.1, Chitin2.9-PSA21.0, and Chitin8.0-PSA20.8. Obviously, these Chitin-g-P(VBO-co-SA) copolymers show a circular scatting halo, indicating the presence of an ordered structure. Interestingly, the SAXS intensity profiles of Chitin0.9-PSA22.1, Chitin2.9-PSA21.0, and Chitin8.0-PSA20.8 show a broad peak centered at 1.656 nm−1 (q max), indicating that the interdomain distance is 3.8 nm according to d = 2π/q max. It should be noted that the chitin backbones could not aggregate to form microphase-separated clusters due to the densely grafted side chains. Therefore, the observed order structure can be ascribed to the local aggregation of fatty acid chains from VBO repeating units, which is consistent with previously reported results [68].

Monotonic tensile tests were carried out to investigate the mechanical properties of Chitin-g-P(VBO-co-SA) copolymers. The stress-strain curves of Chitin-g-P(VBO-co-SA) copolymers prepared with different VBO/SA feed ratios but similar chitin contents are plotted in Fig. 5a. The ultimate tensile strength increases from 0.14 MPa (Chitin0.8-PSA20.2) to 11.7 MPa (Chitin0.8-PSA29.7) rapidly with the increase of PSA content. Nevertheless, the elongation at break decreases from 691 % (Chitin0.8-PSA20.2) to 20 % (Chitin0.8-PSA29.7) due to the gradually increased PSA content. The inset in Fig. 5a shows that a thin transparent Chitin0.9-PSA22.1 specimen can hold a weight of 200 g easily, indicating good mechanical properties. As presented in Fig. 5b, the Young's modulus jumps dramatically from 0.4 MPa for Chitin0.8-PSA20.2 to 94.3 MPa for Chitin0.8-PSA29.7 as the PSA content increases. Therefore, it can be found that the ultimate tensile strength, extensibility, and Young's modulus of Chitin-g-P(VBO-co-SA) copolymers prepared with different VBO/SA feed ratios are mainly controlled by the PSA content. Fig. 5c presents the stress-strain curves of Chitin-g-P(VBO-co-SA) copolymers prepared with the same VBO/SA feed ratio but different chitin contents. The ultimate tensile strength increases from 1.6 MPa (Chitin0.9-PSA22.1) to 8.9 MPa (Chitin8.0-PSA20.8) as the chitin content increases, while the elongation at break decreases from 172 % (Chitin0.9-PSA22.1) to 18 % (Chitin8.0-PSA20.8). Polystyrene-block-polyisoprene-block-polystyrene (SIS) and polystyrene-block-polybutadiene-block-polystyrene (SBS) are commercial elastomers that have been widely used in different fields. SIS (M n = 115 kg/mol, 19 wt% polystyrene) and SBS (M n = 115 kg/mol, 39.5 wt% polystyrene) exhibit ultimate tensile strength of 9.1 and 10.5 MPa, respectively [69,70]. The above results suggest that the mechanical properties of Chitin-g-P(VBO-co-SA) copolymers are close to these commercial elastomers and have the great potential in actual applications. As plotted in Fig. 5d, the Young's modulus values for Chitin0.9-PSA22.1, Chitin2.9-PSA21.0, and Chitin8.0-PSA20.8 are 57.0, 62.3, and 77.4 MPa, respectively. As shown in Fig. S4, the linear P(VBO-co-SA) copolymer counterpart prepared with the same VBO/SA feed ratio exhibits decreased ultimate tensile strength of 1.5 MPa, lower Young's modulus, but increased elongation at break of 262 %, further demonstrating that the chitin content also plays an important role in the macroscopic performance of such kind of chitin-based graft copolymers. The detailed mechanical properties of Chitin-g-P(VBO-co-SA) copolymers are summarized in Table 2 . From the above results, it can be inferred that the mechanical performance of chitin graft copolymers can be systematically adjusted by tuning the VBO/SA feed ratios and chitin contents during RAFT polymerization.

Lignin derivatives have great potential for UV-shielding applications owing to their abundant aromatic functional groups [71]. Chitin-g-P(VBO-co-SA) copolymers also can be used as UV-shielding materials because of the presence of plentiful SA repeating units. These SA segments contain various chromophore groups, including benzene rings, carbonyl groups, aldehyde groups, and methoxyl groups, all of which contribute to UV absorption, resulting in excellent UV-shielding performance [72]. As displayed in Fig. 6a, Chitin0.9-PSA22.1 films with different shapes were placed on UV sensor cards and irradiated with 365 nm UV light for 30 s. After the removal of UV light and Chitin0.9-PSA22.1 films, the UV cards exhibited distinct white areas where covered with the Chitin0.9-PSA22.1 films and purple in the areas without covering the Chitin0.9-PSA22.1 film, demonstrating superior UV-blocking property. To clarify the UV-shielding behavior more clearly, UV–vis measurements were performed on these Chitin-g-P(VBO-co-SA) copolymers as presented in Fig. 6b. It can be found that the UV light in the regions of UVB (280–320 nm) and UVC (200–280 nm) can be completely absorbed by the Chitin-g-P(VBO-co-SA) copolymers. Even in the UVA region (320–400 nm), approximately 70 % UV light (with reference to 400 nm) can be absorbed by the Chitin0.8-PSA20.2 film. Note that the UV absorption is strongly related to the PSA content in the copolymer, which indicates that the UV-shielding performance of these Chitin-g-P(VBO-co-SA) copolymers can be further adjusted by tuning the composition during the polymerization.

To improve the mechanical properties of Chitin-g-P(VBO-co-SA) copolymers, CDs were incorporated as the nanofillers into Chitin0.9-PSA21.7 via coprecipitation. Chitin0.9-PSA21.7/CD composites contain 2, 4, 6 wt% CDs were named as Chitin0.9-PSA21.7@CD2, Chitin0.9-PSA21.7@CD4, and Chitin0.9-PSA21.7@CD6, respectively. The DLS result shown in Fig. S5 indicates that the average diameter of CDs was about 90 nm with the particle-size distribution of 0.61. Specifically, the aldehyde groups in SA repeating units can react with the amino groups on the surface of CDs to form a dynamic network through Schiff-base chemistry. Fig. 7a presents the stress-strain curves of Chitin0.9-PSA21.7@CD composites with different CD contents, and it can be found that the mechanical property of Chitin0.9-PSA21.7 was significantly enhanced after the introduction of CDs. The ultimate tensile strength values for Chitin0.9-PSA21.7@CD2, Chitin0.9-PSA21.7@CD4, and Chitin0.9-PSA21.7@CD6 are 2.7, 4.3, 5.4 MPa respectively, which are much higher than that of Chitin0.9-PSA21.7 (0.7 MPa). Nevertheless, the strain at break decreases from 335 % for Chitin0.9-PSA21.7 to 82 % for Chitin0.9-PSA21.7@CD6. As shown in Fig. 7b, the Young's modulus rises from 0.9 to 93.1 MPa with the increase of CD content in Chitin0.9-PSA21.7, implying that CDs are ideal nanofillers to promote the mechanical performance of these chitin graft copolymers. As plotted in Fig. 7c, Chitin0.9-PSA21.7@CD4 exhibits the highest toughness of 9.2 MJ/m3 by balancing the tensile strength and stretchability, which is almost 6.6 times that of Chitin0.9-PSA21.7 (1.4 MJ/m3). The detailed mechanical properties of Chitin0.9-PSA21.7@CD composites are summarized in Table 3 . Fig. 7d displays the DSC heat flow curves of Chitin0.9-PSA21.7 and Chitin0.9-PSA21.7@CD composites. Note that the T g values for Chitin0.9-PSA21.7, Chitin0.9-PSA21.7@CD2, Chitin0.9-PSA21.7@CD4, and Chitin0.9-PSA21.7@CD6 are 30.9, 40.8, 44.8, 45.2 °C, respectively. Note that T g increases with the increase of CD content in Chitin0.9-PSA21.7, indicating that the incorporated CDs can effectively hinder the movement of side chains caused by the physical hydrogen bonding interaction and Schiff-base network formed by CDs and the chitin graft copolymer matrix, leading to the enhancement of mechanical properties.

The introduction of CDs in Chitin-g-P(VBO-co-SA) copolymers can not only improve their mechanical performance but also can endow them with outstanding photothermal property. Fig. 8a exhibits the changes of temperature as a function of time under NIR laser (808 nm, 2 W/cm2, 1 cm × 1 cm) at room temperature for Chitin0.9-PSA21.7@CD composite films. Noticeably, with 30 s and 39 s NIR irradiation, the temperatures of Chitin0.9-PSA21.7@CD2 and Chitin0.9-PSA21.7@CD4 can grow from 27.0 to 57.1 and 72.0 °C, respectively. For Chitin0.9-PSA21.7@CD6, the temperature rises from 27.0 to 85.9 °C within 55 s, demonstrating excellent photothermal property. To examine the photostability, the composite films were stimulated for 10 on/off switching cycles with NIR irradiation as shown in Fig. 8b-d. It can be found that the temperature rises gradually when the NIR laser is on and drops to room temperature when the NIR laser is off, which implies that these composite films possess good photostability and can be irradiated multiple times without losing their photothermal efficiency. To visually illustrate the photothermal behavior of Chitin0.9-PSA21.7@CD composites, the composite films were irradiated under NIR laser, and the IR images were taken at different irradiation times as depicted in Fig. 8e-g. From the above results, it can be inferred that CDs are potential nanofillers for developing high-performance composite elastomers with extraordinary photothermal and mechanical properties.

In summary, a series of sustainable Chitin-g-P(VBO-co-SA) copolymers with varied compositions and tunable properties were successfully synthesized by changing the PSA and chitin contents during RAFT polymerization. These Chitin-g-P(VBO-co-SA) copolymers are thermally stable and possess good UV-shielding performance. Meanwhile, the mechanical properties of Chitin-g-P(VBO-co-SA) copolymers can be significantly enhanced by introducing CDs as nanofillers, which is attributed to the network formed by the hydrogen bonding and Schiff-base reaction between CDs and the chitin graft copolymer matrix. The incorporation of CDs also gives Chitin-g-P(VBO-co-SA) copolymers outstanding photothermal performance, broadening their applications in noncontact stimuli-responsive materials. Therefore, high-performance functional sustainable elastomers can be achieved by combining chitin, lignin, and plant oil. This versatile and robust strategy can open new avenues for the development of high-value-added renewable materials derived from natural resources.
